enough: NAD is made from vitamin B3 forms: niacin, nicotinamide, and tryptophan* can feed into it. NR and NMN are NAD precursors too, just different entry points.
For most people, getting the normal daily amount of vitamin B3 is generally enough for basic NAD needs. High-dose NR or NMN for "longevity" is still an open question as of June 2026.
*Tryptophan: No, tryptophan is not a vitamin, but rather an essential amino acid. However, your body can use tryptophan as a building block to synthesize Vitamin B3 (known as niacin).

tooHigh: Diets high in saturated fat tend to raise LDL cholesterol and are linked with higher cardiovascular risk when they displace unsaturated fats.
Major health groups recommend keeping saturated fat moderate and swapping toward mono- and polyunsaturated sources.
enough: Some saturated fat is normal in whole foods (dairy, meat, coconut); balance matters more than zero intake.
Pairing saturated fat with fiber-rich plants and unsaturated oils supports a heart-healthier overall pattern.

tooLow: Very low monounsaturated intake often means relying on refined carbs or saturated fats instead of olive- or avocado-style fats.
Mediterranean-style eating patterns linked to heart health emphasize this fat class.
enough: Monounsaturated fats support HDL, help replace saturated and trans fats, and fit well in stable cooking oils.

Extra-virgin olive oil contains DMB (3,3-dimethyl-1-butanol), which in animal studies inhibits gut bacteria from converting choline and carnitine into TMAO—one reason Mediterranean diets correlate with lower TMAO.

tooLow: Too little polyunsaturated fat can mean inadequate essential omega-3 and omega-6 from food.
Very low-fat diets sometimes reduce absorption of fat-soluble vitamins.
enough: Polyunsaturated fats include essential fatty acids the body cannot make in needed amounts.
Balance between omega-3 and omega-6 matters more than total PUFA alone.

tooHigh: Industrial trans fats raise LDL, lower HDL, and increase heart disease risk; there is no safe intake level recommended.
Partially hydrogenated oils in older packaged foods were the main source; still check labels for "partially hydrogenated."
enough: Near-zero industrial trans fat is the goal; tiny amounts can occur naturally in dairy and meat.
Avoiding deep-fried and highly processed baked goods keeps intake minimal.

tooHigh: High dietary cholesterol may raise LDL in some people, especially with low fiber and high saturated fat overall.
For many, saturated and trans fats affect blood lipids more than cholesterol from eggs and shellfish alone.
enough: Dietary cholesterol supports cell membranes and hormone synthesis; needs are partly met by the body.
Whole-food sources (eggs, shellfish) differ from cholesterol paired with processed meats.

tooLow: Low omega-3 intake is linked with higher inflammation markers and weaker cardiovascular support.
Plant ALA converts poorly to EPA/DHA; fish or algae sources matter for direct EPA/DHA.
enough: Omega-3 fats support heart rhythm, triglycerides, brain structure, and resolution of inflammation.
Combine fatty fish, walnuts, flax, or algae oil based on diet preference.

tooHigh: Modern diets often oversupply omega-6 from refined seed oils, skewing the omega-6 : omega-3 ratio.
High omega-6 relative to omega-3 may favor pro-inflammatory eicosanoids when not balanced.
enough: Linoleic acid is essential; the issue is usually excess processed oils, not whole nuts and seeds.
Lowering omega-6-heavy oils while raising omega-3 improves the ratio without eliminating omega-6.

tooLow: Low omega-9 is uncommon; the body can make oleic acid, but food sources add easy unsaturated fat.
Diets very low in all fats may lack oleic-rich staples like olive oil.
enough: Omega-9 (oleic acid) is non-essential but supports replacing saturated fat in cooking.
Olive-oil-forward patterns align with Mediterranean cardiometabolic benefits.

tooLow: Low ALA means fewer plant-based omega-3 building blocks; conversion to EPA/DHA is limited.
Vegans and low-nut/seed diets should plan ALA or direct EPA/DHA from algae.
enough: ALA from flax, chia, and walnuts contributes to omega-3 status alongside or instead of fish.
A few grams daily of ALA-rich foods supports essential fatty acid intake.

tooLow: Low EPA limits anti-inflammatory marine omega-3 effects and may leave triglycerides and mood support under-addressed.
Fish-oil and fatty-fish intake are the main food sources.
enough: EPA supports cardiovascular health, inflammation balance, and is often paired with DHA in fish.
Regular fatty-fish meals or targeted fish oil supply meaningful EPA.
adhd: EPA and DHA tune the phospholipid structure of neuronal membranes—governing how efficiently neurotransmitters bind receptors and move across synapses.

EPA specifically helps reduce peripheral neuroinflammation and supports membrane fluidity around dopamine transporters. Clinical ADHD work often emphasizes high-EPA fish or algae oil (roughly 1,000–2,000 mg EPA daily) over high-DHA alone.
Low omega-3 status correlates with weaker working memory, executive dysfunction, and emotional dysregulation on top of baseline ADHD.
antiAging: EPA dampens inflammatory eicosanoids that drive vascular aging and joint degradation—part of the senomorphic side of longevity nutrition.
Adequate marine omega-3 intake is linked with slower telomere attrition and lower cardiovascular mortality in long-term cohort studies.
cataractsPrevention: EPA helps balance inflammatory eicosanoids in retinal tissue; higher marine omega-3 intake is associated with lower cataract risk in several observational studies.
Fatty fish and algae oil supply EPA alongside DHA for the full omega-3 eye-support stack.

tooLow: Low DHA may affect brain and retina structure over time; important in pregnancy and aging.
Plant ALA alone often does not restore DHA without marine or algae sources.
enough: DHA is a major structural fat in the brain and eyes and supports cognitive and visual health.
Fatty fish and algae-based supplements provide direct DHA.
adhd: DHA is a major structural omega-3 in the brain; together with EPA it optimizes membrane signaling for attention and mood circuits.

While DHA is critical for long-term brain structure, high-EPA formulations have shown more consistent benefit for ADHD symptom clusters in trials—many clinicians pair both via fatty fish or fish/algae oil.
Algae-based oils are the main direct food or supplement route for DHA when fish intake is low.
antiAging: DHA maintains neuronal membrane structure and supports brain resilience as oxidative stress rises with age.
Higher DHA status correlates with slower cognitive decline and better retinal protection—two hallmark targets of healthy aging.
cataractsPrevention: DHA is a major structural fat in the retina and helps maintain cell-membrane fluidity under oxidative stress—relevant to long-term lens and retinal health.
Salmon, sardines, herring, and algae DHA are direct food sources when plant ALA alone is insufficient.

tooHigh: Excess linoleic acid from refined oils can dominate omega-6 intake and widen the omega-6 : omega-3 gap.
Ultra-processed foods fried in soybean or corn oil are common drivers.
enough: Linoleic acid is an essential omega-6; whole-food sources (nuts, seeds) differ from bottled oils used everywhere.
Aim for adequacy without high-omega-6 oils crowding out omega-3.

tooHigh: Arachidonic acid can feed pro-inflammatory pathways when omega-3 is low and overall diet is heavy in certain animal fats.
It is less commonly tracked than total omega-6 but appears in meat and eggs.
enough: Some arachidonic acid is normal in omnivorous diets; balance with omega-3 and whole plants matters.
Not essential to maximize; focus on ratio and food quality.

tooLow: Low oleic acid often reflects little olive or avocado oil use—not usually a clinical deficiency.
Replacing butter or palm oil with oleic-rich oils improves fat quality.
enough: Oleic acid is the main omega-9 in olive oil and supports heart-healthy fat substitution.
Stable for cooking compared with high-PUFA oils.

tooHigh: Diets heavy in overfed animal fats may supply excess palmitoleic acid (omega-7), which some research links with liver fat and metabolic stress when overall diet quality is poor.
The bigger risk is usually the surrounding pattern—processed meats, fried foods, and low omega-3—not this single fatty acid in isolation.
enough: Small amounts of omega-7 from fish, macadamia, and sea buckthorn fit a normal diet without needing to chase high intake.
Focus on whole-food fat quality and omega balance rather than maximizing omega-7.

tooLow: Low vitamin E reduces antioxidant protection for cell membranes and LDL particles.
Very low-fat diets or fat malabsorption increase risk.
enough: Vitamin E protects fats from oxidation and supports immune and skin health.
Nuts, seeds, and oils are the best food sources; needs rise with high PUFA intake.
antiAging: Vitamin E shields cell membranes and LDL from lipid peroxidation—the oxidative damage that accelerates vascular and brain aging.
It works as an antioxidant shield alongside vitamin C; low E leaves fats in membranes and mitochondria vulnerable to free-radical chain reactions.
cataractsPrevention: Vitamin E protects the lipid-rich membranes of eye tissues from oxidation; population studies link higher E intake from nuts and seeds with lower cataract rates.
It pairs with vitamin C in the lens antioxidant network—food E from almonds, sunflower seeds, and leafy greens is preferable to high-dose pills.

tooLow: Low vitamin K can impair clotting factors and osteocalcin activity, affecting bone and vascular calcium handling.
Antibiotics, warfarin, or very low green vegetable intake increase risk.
enough: Vitamin K supports blood clotting and helps direct calcium toward bone rather than soft tissues.
K1 from greens and K2 from fermented foods and some animal products play overlapping roles.

tooLow: Low K1 (phylloquinone) mainly affects clotting and bone proteins when green vegetable intake is very low.
Consistent leafy greens usually cover K1.
enough: K1 from plants supports clotting and works with vitamin D and calcium for bone metabolism.
A serving of leafy greens most days is a simple baseline.

tooLow: Low K2 (menaquinone) is debated for bone and arterial health, especially with high calcium and low K overall.
Fermented foods and animal products supply most dietary K2.
enough: K2 may help activate proteins that regulate where calcium deposits; research is strongest in deficiency and CKD contexts.
Natto, cheese, and egg yolks are notable sources.

tooLow: Low MK-4 from meats and dairy may leave short gaps between meals because it clears from the body within hours.
Plant-forward diets without eggs or animal products often have little dietary MK-4.
enough: MK-4 from animal foods supports K-dependent proteins that route calcium toward bone.
Spread intake across meals when possible—MK-4 does not linger like MK-7.

tooLow: Low MK-7 is common without natto, aged cheese, or an MK-7 supplement—many natto products have K2 removed.
Without MK-7, you may miss the menaquinone form best suited to once-daily supplementation.
enough: MK-7 from fermented foods has a half-life of days, supporting steady K2 status from one daily source.
For supplements, choose MK-7 specifically rather than unspecified K2.

tooLow: Low selenium weakens antioxidant enzymes (glutathione peroxidase), thyroid conversion, and immunity.
Soil-poor regions and very restrictive diets increase risk.
enough: Selenium supports thyroid hormone activation, DNA repair, and oxidative stress defense.
A few Brazil nuts weekly or seafood and eggs often cover needs—avoid megadoses.
antiAging: Selenium powers glutathione peroxidase and other antioxidant enzymes that neutralize peroxides before they damage DNA.
Adequate selenium supports thyroid hormone activation and DNA repair—two systems that drift with age when trace intake is low.
hairLoss: Selenium supports thyroid hormone activation and glutathione peroxidase—both tie into hair cycling; deficiency can worsen shedding, but excess selenium is also toxic to hair.
A few Brazil nuts weekly, seafood, or eggs usually cover needs without megadose supplements.
cataractsPrevention: Selenium powers glutathione peroxidase in the lens and other eye tissues, helping neutralize peroxides that drive cataract-related oxidative damage.
Brazil nuts, seafood, and eggs supply trace selenium; avoid daily handfuls of Brazil nuts, which can overshoot safe intake.

dashboardTracking: On this dashboard, track copper against its own daily value (% DV)—that is the primary signal.
Copper supports iron metabolism; low copper can look like iron deficiency even when iron intake is adequate. Watch both nutrients on their own % DV bars, not as a combined ratio.
Chronic high-dose zinc is what most often depletes copper—not iron from food.
tooLow: Low copper can weaken connective tissue, iron metabolism, and antioxidant enzymes over time.
Very high zinc intake without balance can interfere with copper absorption.
enough: Copper supports collagen cross-linking, energy production, and iron transport.
Whole-food sources usually cover needs; megadoses are unnecessary and can be harmful.

tooLow: Very low methionine intake usually reflects too little total high-quality protein, which can limit methylation chemistry, enzyme building, liver detox pathways, and tissue repair.
Restrictive diets with low protein variety may miss sulfur amino acids unless legumes, soy, seeds, grains, eggs, dairy, fish, or meat are planned intentionally.
enough: Methionine is an essential sulfur amino acid from protein-rich food. Your body uses it with ATP to make SAMe (S-adenosyl-L-methionine), the methyl donor HNMT uses to metabolize intracellular histamine.
Methionine also feeds the methylation cycle that connects SAMe, homocysteine, folate, B12, B6, riboflavin, and glutathione-related sulfur metabolism.
tooHigh: High methionine without enough folate, B12, B6, and riboflavin can raise homocysteine pressure in susceptible people.
The target is adequate protein and methylation cofactors, not isolated high-dose methionine supplements.

tooLow: Low polyphenol intake means fewer plant antioxidants that support gut microbes, blood vessels, and inflammation balance.
Diets low in berries, tea, cocoa, and herbs miss easy sources.
enough: Polyphenols from diverse plants support blood vessel flexibility, gut microbial diversity, and lower oxidative stress—patterns linked with lower cardiovascular and cognitive decline risk over decades.
They also act as a TMAO protector: polyphenols feed beneficial microbes and curb the gut bacteria that turn choline and carnitine into TMA, the precursor of atherosclerosis-linked TMAO.
Regular berries, tea, cocoa, herbs, and olive oil add protective phytochemicals that vitamins alone do not replace.
antiAging: Polyphenols act as senomorphics—they quiet inflammatory signaling in aging cells without directly killing senescent cells.
Diverse plant polyphenols support gut microbes that produce protective metabolites and lower the oxidative stress that damages DNA over decades.

tooLow: Low flavonoid intake parallels low fruit, tea, and vegetable diversity—linked with weaker vascular support in population studies.
Highly processed diets often lack these compounds entirely.
enough: Flavonoids (a polyphenol subclass) support endothelial function and may modestly lower blood pressure in higher intakes.

As a TMAO protector, flavonoids from berries, tea, citrus, and onions help shift the microbiome away from the bacteria that produce TMA—part of why plant-rich eaters tend to have lower TMAO and less atherosclerosis risk.
Citrus, berries, tea, and onions are reliable contributors.
antiAging: Flavonoids such as quercetin (onions, apples, tea) are studied as senomorphics and senolytics in supplement trials—food intake supports antioxidant and endothelial protection.
Regular berries, citrus, tea, and onions supply the flavonoid diversity that helps cells resist oxidative DNA damage over decades.

tooLow: Low carotenoids reduce beta-carotene, lutein, and lycopene intake—important for eyes, skin, and antioxidant networks.
Very low produce intake is the usual cause.
enough: Carotenoids from orange, red, and green plants support vision (lutein/zeaxanthin) and general antioxidant status.
Fat with meals improves absorption.
The longevity panel uses ~15 mg/day as a food-pattern reference (not an FDA Daily Value). Carrots, sweet potato, and leafy greens are reliable sources.
cataractsPrevention: Carotenoids—including beta-carotene, lutein, and lycopene—filter short-wavelength light and quench lens oxidants; low produce intake reduces this protective pool.
Orange, red, and green plants with a little fat at meals improve absorption of the fat-soluble carotenoid network.

tooLow: The body cannot synthesize lutein—it must come from diet or supplements.
Low lutein usually means little dark leafy green, yellow-orange produce, or egg yolk—reducing macular and brain carotenoid intake.
enough: Lutein concentrates in the macula with zeaxanthin, filtering blue light and lowering retinal oxidative stress—supporting vision and lowering AMD and cataract risk.
It is the predominant carotenoid in brain tissue and is linked with learning, memory, and cognitive performance across the lifespan.
Antioxidant activity may help limit arterial plaque and protect skin from UV and blue light. As a fat-soluble carotenoid, pair with healthy fats for absorption.
cataractsPrevention: Lutein and zeaxanthin concentrate in the macula and lens, filtering blue light and lowering oxidative stress—population studies link higher lutein intake with lower cataract risk.
Dark leafy greens, egg yolks, and corn cooked with olive oil are practical food sources; pair with fat for absorption.

tooLow: No essential deficiency—low curcumin simply means less turmeric in cooking and fewer curry-style anti-inflammatory phytochemicals.
Whole turmeric with black pepper and fat improves absorption vs. spice dust alone.
enough: Curcumin from turmeric is studied for joint comfort and inflammation markers; food amounts are smaller than trial doses.
Regular culinary turmeric plus pepper is a low-risk way to include it.
antiAging: Curcumin from turmeric is studied both as a senomorphic (NF-κB and inflammatory cytokine modulation) and as a senolytic in high-dose supplement trials—not at typical food amounts.
Culinary turmeric with pepper and fat is a low-risk senomorphic habit; supplement curcumin is what most aging research doses for stronger effects.

tooLow: Not an essential nutrient—low resveratrol reflects little red grapes, berries, or peanuts in the diet.
Wine is not required; grapes and berries supply it without alcohol.
enough: Resveratrol is a polyphenol studied for aging pathways; food doses are far below many supplement trials.

In animal studies it lowers TMAO by remodeling gut bacteria (raising Akkermansia and bile-acid signaling), one of the microbiome-shifting protectors against atherosclerosis—human food doses are modest, so treat it as one of several plant compounds, not a standalone fix.
Whole grapes, blueberries, and peanuts contribute modest amounts.
antiAging: Resveratrol activates stress-response pathways (sirtuins) studied in aging biology and appears in senolytic supplement protocols alongside quercetin and fisetin.
Food amounts from grapes and berries provide senomorphic antioxidant support; trial doses are usually far higher from supplements.

tooLow: CoQ10 is made in the body; low food intake rarely causes deficiency except with statins or certain diseases.
Older age and some medications lower tissue CoQ10.
enough: CoQ10 supports mitochondrial energy and may help when depleted (e.g., on statins).
Organ meats and fatty fish provide small dietary amounts.
antiAging: CoQ10 is the mitochondrial electron shuttle between complexes I/II and III—tissue levels fall with age and statin use, reducing cellular energy capacity.
Adequate CoQ10 from organ meats, fish, and nuts supports ATP production and may blunt the oxidative stress that damages mitochondrial DNA over time.

tooLow: Taurine is synthesized in the body, but blood and tissue levels fall with age; strict plant-based diets supply little from food.
Low intake is rarely a classical deficiency, but animal-rich foods are the practical way to offset age-related decline.
enough: Taurine supports bile acid conjugation, heart muscle function, retinal health, and acts as a mitochondrial antioxidant.
Shellfish, dark poultry meat, beef, fish, eggs, and dairy are the richest food sources.
antiAging: Taurine levels decline significantly with age; a 2023 Science study showed taurine supplementation extended lifespan in mice and improved healthspan markers (bone density, muscle, immune function).
Acts as a mitochondrial antioxidant and supports bile acid metabolism—both decline with aging. Human trials are ongoing; food-first shellfish, dark poultry, and fish are the practical targets.

tooLow: No classical deficiency—low sulforaphane means infrequent cruciferous vegetables, missing easy detox-support phytochemicals.
Chopping broccoli and letting it sit before cooking boosts sulforaphane formation.
enough: Sulforaphane from broccoli sprouts and crucifers activates cellular antioxidant defenses (Nrf2).
Several weekly servings of broccoli, kale, or cabbage are practical targets.

tooHigh: Very high choline intake—especially from frequent eggs, liver, and red meat—supplies substrate for gut bacteria to make TMAO (trimethylamine N-oxide), which is associated with higher atherosclerosis and cardiovascular event risk in observational studies.
This does not mean avoiding eggs entirely; risk rises with heavy animal-product patterns and may be lower when fiber-rich plants and fish-forward diets support a different gut microbiome.
enough: Choline supports brain development, liver fat export, and cell membranes; adequate intake prevents deficiency without needing megadoses.
To lower TMAO: rotate egg and liver portions, emphasize plants and fish, and support gut microbes with fiber—vegetarians often produce far less TMAO even when choline is adequate.

tooHigh: L-carnitine is abundant in red meat; gut microbes convert it to TMAO more efficiently than they do choline from plants—one reason heavy red-meat diets correlate with higher TMAO and cardiovascular risk in research.
Fish and chicken supply far less carnitine than beef and lamb; plant-based eaters typically have very low dietary carnitine (the body makes some on its own).
enough: Carnitine helps shuttle fatty acids into mitochondria for energy; deficiency is rare except in specific medical conditions.
To lower TMAO: cut back red meat frequency, add fiber-rich plants, use olive oil, and keep vitamin D and B vitamins adequate—see the TMAO balance section.

tooHigh: Betaine (trimethylglycine) is another TMAO precursor—gut bacteria convert it to TMA like they do choline and carnitine.
High intake from wheat bran, beets, and betaine supplements (sometimes marketed for homocysteine) can feed TMAO production when paired with a meat-heavy microbiome.
enough: Betaine supports methylation and liver health in normal food amounts; you do not need to supplement it for TMAO reasons.
If homocysteine is a concern, folate and B12 often help without the TMAO trade-off of high-dose betaine.

tooHigh: Phosphorus is essential for bones, teeth, and cellular energy (ATP), but chronically high blood phosphate is the problem—not the mineral itself in normal whole foods.

When phosphate stays elevated, the body releases parathyroid hormone and FGF23, suppresses active vitamin D, and can pull calcium out of bone while promoting calcium-phosphate deposits in artery walls—vascular calcification that stiffens vessels and raises heart attack and stroke risk.

Phosphate additives in cola (phosphoric acid), processed cheese, deli meat, and fast food are absorbed far more efficiently than phosphorus bound in whole foods—often 90%+ vs roughly half from natural sources—so they spike blood phosphate without you noticing.
People with kidney disease must limit phosphorus carefully; even in healthy adults, the modern pattern is usually too much additive phosphate, not too little.
enough: From meat, dairy, beans, and nuts, phosphorus supports strong bones and every cell's energy metabolism; deficiency is rare without serious illness.
Whole-food phosphorus rarely needs chasing—adequate protein and legumes cover needs. The prevention target is avoiding excess absorbable phosphate from additives, not maximizing intake.

tooHigh: Frequent high-GI choices spike blood glucose quickly and can increase glycemic load when portions are large.
White bread, sugary drinks, and refined cereals are common high-GI staples.
enough: The dashboard shows a carb-weighted average GI from your matched foods—lower is better (≤55 low, 56–69 medium, ≥70 high).
Lower-GI carbs digest slower, smoothing glucose and insulin and reducing post-meal crashes that drive cravings and overeating.
Choosing slower carbs over white bread, sugary drinks, and refined cereals helps prevent insulin resistance and fatty liver when portions stay reasonable.
foodSources: Low: beans, pasta al dente, apples
Medium: oats, sweet potato, brown rice
High: white bread, rice cakes, candy

tooHigh: High added sugar drives glycemic load, liver fat, triglycerides, and dental issues; linked with weight gain when calories exceed needs.
Soda, sweets, and sweetened yogurt are concentrated sources beyond whole fruit.
enough: Keeping added sugar modest leaves room for whole fruit and stable energy.
WHO suggests limiting free sugars for cardiometabolic health; labels list "added sugars" separately.

tooHigh: Heavy refined carb intake (white flour, stripped grains) raises glycemic load and often displaces fiber and micronutrients.
Frequent spikes can worsen insulin resistance over time in susceptible people.
enough: Whole grains and intact grains digest slower and carry more fiber and minerals.
Mixing refined carbs with protein, fat, and vegetables blunts glucose response.

tooHigh: Ratios well above 4:1 (often 10:1+ in Western diets) suggest omega-6-heavy oils dominating omega-3 intake.
Shifting oils toward olive oil and adding fish or flax improves the ratio without eliminating omega-6.
enough: A lower omega-6 : omega-3 ratio is associated with less inflammatory signaling, which may lower long-term risk of heart disease, joint problems, and cognitive decline.
Shifting from soybean and corn oil toward olive oil, fish, walnuts, and flax is one of the highest-leverage fat changes for inflammation balance.

tooHigh: A high saturated : unsaturated ratio mirrors butter, palm, and fatty meat displacing olive oil, nuts, and fish.
Heart-health guidelines emphasize replacing saturated with unsaturated fats.
enough: More unsaturated fat relative to saturated supports healthier lipid profiles in most people.
Cook with olive or avocado oil; keep saturated fat from whole foods in moderation.
foodSources: Lower ratio: olive oil, fish, nuts
Higher ratio: butter, cheese, palm oil

tooLow: Low combined EPA + DHA limits direct marine omega-3 benefits for heart and brain.
Flax alone may not raise EPA/DHA blood levels enough for many people.
enough: EPA and DHA together protect heart rhythm, lower triglycerides, support brain structure, and help resolve inflammation after injury or illness.
Most adults benefit from roughly 250–500 mg/day combined from fatty fish or algae oil—enough to matter for prevention, not just deficiency.

targetReference: Actual ratio = vitamin E (mg/day) ÷ PUFA (g/day) → mg alpha-tocopherol per g PUFA.
% of target = actual ratio ÷ 0.6 mg/g target. At 100%, your logged vitamin E matches your logged PUFA at the review ratio.

The reference range is ~0.4–0.6 mg vitamin E per gram of PUFA overall, with omega-6 around ~0.5 mg/g and omega-3 fish oils around ~0.6–1 mg/g; monounsaturated and saturated fats add no extra requirement.
As PUFA rises, vitamin E matters more, and vitamin C, selenium, polyphenols, and carotenoids help recycle or extend that antioxidant cover.
tooLow: Vitamin E below about 0.6 mg alpha-tocopherol per gram of PUFA leaves fragile fats with less antioxidant cover, especially when those fats come from refined or heated oils.
High PUFA from fried foods, old bottles, or fish oil can add oxidative stress over time when it is not paired with enough vitamin E-rich whole foods and supporting antioxidants.
enough: At 100%+ of target, your logged vitamin E covers your logged PUFA at the review ratio. That is a useful dietary pattern check, not a guarantee against rancid oils.
This match helps protect cell membranes, LDL particles, dietary fats, mitochondria, and DNA from lipid peroxidation and related oxidative stress.

Whole foods like sunflower seeds, almonds, hazelnuts, and avocado often pair PUFA with vitamin E, while vitamin C regenerates vitamin E, selenium supports glutathione enzymes, and polyphenols and carotenoids add broader antioxidant cover.

targetReference: Two different scales get mixed up online. Per serving or per meal: GL ≤10 is low, 11–19 moderate, ≥20 high — so a GL of 80 for one meal would be very high. This app tracks your daily average across the whole week.

For daily totals, common guides use roughly <100 GL/day as favorable, 100–120 moderate, and >120 high. The % target bar uses 100 as that daily reference — not a vitamin % DV and not a blood glucose reading.

Some programs cite ~80 GL/day on a 2000 kcal diet as a moderate target when stepping down from a high-carb pattern; others use <80/day as a strict low-GL diet definition. There is no single official cutoff — portion size and individual metabolism still matter.
tooHigh: High daily glycemic load means your week is built on large portions of fast-digesting carbs—white rice, bread, sweets, and sugary drinks.
Over years this pattern raises risk of type 2 diabetes, fatty liver, cardiovascular disease, and the energy crashes that make snacking hard to control.
enough: Moderate glycemic load keeps blood sugar steadier, which protects arteries, supports weight stability, and reduces afternoon fatigue.
Beans, intact grains, vegetables, and smaller starch portions are the practical prevention lever—portion size often matters more than any single GI number.
Micronutrients from your food log are repeated here on purpose—longevity is not separate from vitamins, minerals, and fiber, and seeing them beside fats and compounds helps you spot gaps.
Aim nutrients (fiber, potassium, magnesium, D, B vitamins, iodine) support decades-long outcomes: blood pressure, bones, thyroid, immunity, and gut health.
Sodium is the main watch item—high % DV from processed and restaurant food raises stroke and kidney risk even when everything else looks fine.

Ratios summarize whether your fat pattern is inflammatory or protective—the omega-6 : omega-3 and saturated : unsaturated scores reflect heart and joint health risk over decades, not just today's numbers.
EPA + DHA combined captures the marine omega-3 dose most tied to lower triglycerides, steadier mood, and brain aging resilience.
Near-zero trans fat is a hard prevention target: even small amounts from fried and packaged foods raise LDL, lower HDL, and increase heart attack risk with no known safe level.
Fiber from your micro entries is highlighted here because colon health is largely shaped by diet and screening—adequate fiber is one of the strongest levers you can track weekly.
High fiber lowers colorectal cancer risk and helps prevent diverticulitis, constipation, and hemorrhoids; low fiber with frequent red meat pushes the other way.
Aim for 100%+ DV (about 38 g/day for men, 25 g/day for women). Insoluble fiber shortens colon transit time; soluble fiber feeds gut bacteria that produce protective butyrate.

tooLow: As you get older—especially after 60—thyroid function is particularly sensitive to variations in iodine intake. Iodine deficiency plays an important role in hypothyroidism: an enlarged thyroid (goiter), slowed metabolism, fatigue, cold intolerance, and cognitive sluggishness.
Low selenium, iron, or zinc can limit how well iodine supports hormone production even when iodine intake looks adequate on paper.
Low omega-3 intake leaves the thyroid more exposed to inflammation and oxidative stress; a high omega-6 : omega-3 ratio from refined seed oils can further skew inflammatory signaling.
Low vitamin A impairs thyroid receptor activation and T4→T3 conversion—iodine alone cannot fully support thyroid function when vitamin A is inadequate.
enough: Iodine is the main nutrient to track for thyroid health—steady intake from iodized salt, dairy, eggs, and seafood prevents the deficiency pattern that becomes more consequential after 60.

Iodine is not the whole story: selenium helps convert T4 to active T3 and protects the thyroid gland during hormone synthesis; iron supports thyroid peroxidase; zinc is needed for T3 production; tyrosine is the amino-acid building block for thyroid hormones. Track iodine first, then these cofactors if you avoid iodized salt, eat little seafood, or notice low-thyroid signs.

Omega-3 fatty acids are highly important for thyroid health. They reduce inflammation and oxidative stress, which helps protect the thyroid gland from damage. EPA and DHA also produce metabolic compounds called resolvins that are crucial for managing autoimmune conditions like Hashimoto's disease. Additionally, they ensure cell membrane integrity for proper hormone signaling.

Vitamin A is essential for proper thyroid function, largely because it works hand-in-hand with iodine. It helps your body activate thyroid receptors and is required to convert the inactive thyroid hormone T4 into the active energy-boosting form, T3. Without enough Vitamin A, iodine struggles to do its job properly.
Food-level iodine is usually safer than high-dose supplements—excess iodine from megadose seaweed or concentrated pills can also disrupt thyroid function, especially with autoimmune thyroid disease.
tooHigh: Excess iodine from high-dose supplements or daily seaweed megadoses can trigger or worsen thyroid dysfunction—especially in people with autoimmune thyroid disease.
Iodized salt in normal cooking amounts is rarely a problem; watch concentrated supplements and very large seaweed portions.
foodSources: Sweet potato, carrot & leafy greens (vitamin A)

enough: Calcium, vitamin D, and magnesium work together to mineralize bone, maintain muscle strength, and support immune defense—reducing fracture and fall risk as you age.

Spread calcium through the day with enough vitamin D, magnesium, and weight-bearing activity for best lifelong bone outcomes; peak bone mass in youth and slowing loss after menopause both depend on this trio.
This balance matters most after 40, during menopause, with low sun exposure, or when family history includes osteoporosis.
Stress resilience belongs in longevity because chronic stress accelerates cardiovascular aging, sleep disruption, immune drift, and cognitive burnout—not just bad weeks.

Adaptogen herbs and mushrooms—ashwagandha, rhodiola, holy basil, reishi, cordyceps, lion's mane—are studied for HPA-axis modulation and stress tolerance, but active compounds vary by product and dose. This section tracks underlying nutrients from food instead: magnesium, vitamin C, pantothenic acid (B5), B6, folate, B12, zinc, and tyrosine feed adrenal cofactors, catecholamine synthesis, and methylation that help prevent the depletion pattern sometimes called adrenal fatigue.

EPA and DHA, polyphenols, flavonoids, and CoQ10 support inflammatory tone and cellular energy when stress is high. These repeat values from your micro and longevity entries so you can spot diet gaps—not a substitute for medical evaluation of burnout, anxiety disorders, or cortisol disorders.
foodSources: Magnesium — leafy greens, nuts, seeds, legumes
Vitamin C — citrus, peppers, berries, broccoli
B vitamins — whole grains, legumes, eggs, meat
Zinc — oysters, pumpkin seeds, beef, legumes
Adaptogen herbs — ashwagandha, rhodiola, holy basil (tea/supplements)
Adaptogen mushrooms — reishi, cordyceps, lion's mane, shiitake

enough: Sleep health belongs in longevity because quality of life depends on rest: falling asleep easily, staying asleep through the night, and waking refreshed enough to function the next day.

The older you get, uninterrupted sleep usually gets harder—lighter sleep, more nighttime waking, and earlier mornings are common. Nutrition cannot replace sleep hygiene, but gaps in magnesium, tryptophan, glycine, vitamin B6, or vitamin D can make those patterns worse when intake is low.

Magnesium supports muscle relaxation and calmer nervous-system signaling; tryptophan feeds serotonin and melatonin synthesis; glycine acts as an inhibitory neurotransmitter linked to sleep onset; vitamin B6 helps convert tryptophan along that pathway; vitamin D deficiency is linked with poor sleep depth and daytime fatigue. These repeat values from your micro entries so you can spot diet gaps—not a substitute for medical evaluation of insomnia or sleep apnea.
Insulin resistance is when cells respond less well to insulin—blood sugar stays higher, the pancreas pumps more insulin, and over years that pattern raises type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular risk.

Aim nutrients here: magnesium and fiber blunt glucose spikes; vitamin D, zinc, biotin, and chromium support glucose handling when intake is low; EPA and DHA and monounsaturated fat (olive oil, avocado) support a lower-inflammatory fat pattern; polyphenols and flavonoids from berries, tea, cocoa, and vegetables add modest metabolic support.

Watch nutrients: saturated fat, cholesterol, trans fat, excess omega-6, refined carbs, and added sugar—especially when those foods are calorie-dense and dominate the week. That pattern can support fat gain and worsening insulin sensitivity over time.
Use the carb quality and glycemic load sections for GI, daily glycemic load, and refined-carb detail—the carb spike side of this pattern.
foodSources: Magnesium — leafy greens, nuts, seeds, legumes, whole grains
Fiber — beans, berries, vegetables, intact grains
Vitamin D — fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified dairy
Chromium — broccoli, whole grains, brewer's yeast
EPA & DHA — salmon, sardines, mackerel, herring
Monounsaturated fat — olive oil, avocado, almonds
Polyphenols & flavonoids — berries, tea, cocoa, onions, grapes
Lower-insulin-load pattern — beans, berries, intact grains, fish, vegetables
Watch calorie density — fried foods, pastries, processed meats, sugary drinks

enough: Visceral fat is the deep abdominal fat wrapped around organs—not the pinchable fat under the skin. It drives insulin resistance, chronic inflammation, and higher risk of type 2 diabetes, heart disease, and fatty liver over decades.

Nutrients that help reduce visceral fat buildup: fiber improves satiety and gut signaling; protein preserves lean mass and blunts hunger; magnesium, vitamin D, chromium, and leucine support insulin sensitivity and body composition; EPA, DHA, and monounsaturated fat support a lower-inflammatory fat-storage pattern when intake is low.

Antioxidants (vitamin C, E, selenium, polyphenols, flavonoids, carotenoids, curcumin) lower oxidative stress in adipose tissue and liver—oxidative stress worsens where fat gets stored and how easily it is released.

Nutrients that support mobilizing stored fat—including visceral fat—for energy: B vitamins build NAD+, FAD, and coenzyme A for fuel oxidation; magnesium and iron support ATP and cytochromes; carnitine shuttles fatty acids into mitochondria; CoQ10 carries electrons in the respiratory chain. When these run low, fat tends to stay stored instead of being burned.

Gut incretin signaling (GLP-1-like): soluble fiber feeds bacteria that produce short-chain fatty acids and stimulate incretin hormones—research links this pattern to lower visceral adiposity. Polyphenols, flavonoids, and resveratrol from plants further reshape the microbiome toward lower fat storage. Fermented foods help but are not tracked here.

Nutrition supports the pattern—exercise, sleep, and stress management do the rest. This section tracks buildup, antioxidant, mobilization, and incretin-support nutrients from your entries so you can see whether your week supports visceral-fat reduction, not just total weight.
Jump to fat gain and insulin resistance for related metabolic patterns; pair higher fiber and protein with regular movement for the strongest effect.
foodSources: Fiber — beans, lentils, oats, barley, berries, broccoli, chia, flax
Protein — fish, poultry, eggs, Greek yogurt, tofu, legumes
Magnesium — leafy greens, nuts, seeds, legumes, whole grains
Vitamin D — fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified dairy
Chromium — broccoli, whole grains, brewer's yeast
Leucine — chicken, fish, eggs, dairy, soy
EPA & DHA — salmon, sardines, mackerel, herring

Vitamin E — nuts, seeds, avocado, olive oil
Selenium — Brazil nuts, fish, eggs, whole grains

enough: Fat gain belongs in longevity because visceral fat and muscle loss accelerate with age—slower metabolism, lower activity, gradual loss of insulin sensitivity (cells need more insulin over time), poor sleep, and chronic cortisol all push the same direction over decades, not just from overeating.

Nutrients that support fat from aging: fiber and magnesium blunt glucose swings; vitamin D, chromium, and leucine support body composition and muscle retention; EPA, DHA, monounsaturated fat, polyphenols, and flavonoids support a lower-inflammatory fat pattern when intake is low.

Nutrients that support using food for energy rather than storing it as fat: B vitamins build NAD+, FAD, and coenzyme A for fuel oxidation; magnesium and iron support ATP and cytochromes; carnitine shuttles fatty acids into mitochondria; CoQ10 carries electrons in the respiratory chain.

Gut GLP-like compounds: soluble fiber feeds bacteria that produce short-chain fatty acids and stimulate incretin signaling (GLP-1-like effects)—polyphenols, flavonoids, and resveratrol from plants further reshape the microbiome toward lower fat storage. Fermented foods help but are not counted here.
Jump to insulin resistance / sensitivity, sleep health, and stress resilience for related patterns—poor sleep and cortisol both promote fat gain independent of this nutrient list.
foodSources: Fiber — beans, oats, berries, vegetables, intact grains
Leucine — chicken, fish, eggs, dairy, soy
B vitamins — whole grains, legumes, eggs, meat
Carnitine — beef, lamb, fish, dairy (moderate portions)
CoQ10 — organ meats, fatty fish, nuts
Polyphenols & flavonoids — berries, tea, cocoa, onions, grapes

enough: Mitochondrial health is cellular energy health—NAD+, FAD, coenzyme A, and CoQ10 keep the electron transport chain running so ATP can fuel repair, immunity, and cognition.

Niacin (B3) is the main food precursor for NAD+; riboflavin (B2) builds FAD; pantothenic acid (B5) builds coenzyme A; thiamin (B1) feeds pyruvate into the Krebs cycle; magnesium stabilizes ATP; iron supports cytochromes; manganese supports mitochondrial antioxidant defense via manganese superoxide dismutase (MnSOD); CoQ10 shuttles electrons between mitochondrial complexes.
When these run low, cells shift into low-energy metabolism—fatigue, metabolic drift, and faster vascular aging follow over decades, not just on bad days.
Cellular aging is driven partly by chronic inflammation, oxidative DNA damage, and senescent cells that refuse to die cleanly—nutrition mainly helps through senomorphic pathways.
Vitamin D downregulates age-related inflammatory signaling; vitamin C and E neutralize free radicals that damage DNA; selenium and plant polyphenols support antioxidant enzyme systems.

Essential vitamins function like a shield—protecting cells from oxidative stress and DNA damage that triggers aging. That is different from true senolytics (fisetin, quercetin, curcumin, resveratrol in supplement trials) that target senescent cells directly; food patterns support the senomorphic side.
The type of fat you eat shapes LDL and HDL cholesterol, artery inflammation, and long-term heart attack and stroke risk more than total fat alone.
Diets heavy in saturated and industrial trans fat tend to stiffen blood vessels and raise LDL; swapping toward olive oil, nuts, avocado, and fish is one of the strongest dietary prevention moves.
Cholesterol from whole eggs or shellfish is less concerning for many people than the same calories from processed meats paired with refined carbs and low fiber.
This section tracks PUFA against vitamin E and related antioxidants—see the callout above for the core idea (~0.6 mg vitamin E per gram PUFA).
Use Read more for fat-type guidelines, recycling nutrients, cooking and storage, and PUFA + vitamin E food pairings.

Omega-3 and omega-6 fats compete in inflammation pathways—too much omega-6 from fried and packaged foods without enough omega-3 may keep the body in a low-grade inflammatory state linked to heart disease, arthritis, and cognitive aging.
EPA and DHA from fish and algae directly support brain structure, mood stability, and triglyceride control; ALA from flax and walnuts helps but converts only partly.
Improving this balance—more fatty fish and olive oil, less soybean and corn oil—is a practical anti-inflammatory strategy you can measure over weeks.
Glutathione is a tripeptide antioxidant your body makes from cysteine, glycine, and glutamate to help control oxidative stress, immune function, and detoxification.

This section repeats supporting nutrients instead of treating glutathione as a direct food target: vitamin C helps recycle glutathione; riboflavin supports glutathione reductase; selenium powers glutathione peroxidase; vitamin E works alongside the antioxidant network.

Sulfur-rich foods such as onions, garlic, broccoli, Brussels sprouts, cabbage, and other crucifers help supply the sulfur and Nrf2-supporting plant compounds that keep this system active. Protein-rich foods such as lean meats, poultry, eggs, legumes, and seeds supply amino acid building blocks.
DNA repair is not driven by one magic nutrient. The most useful pattern is covering the cofactors for repair enzymes, nucleotide building, methylation, and antioxidant defense.

Vitamin C, vitamin E, selenium, polyphenols, flavonoids, and carotenoids help lower free-radical pressure before oxidative lesions accumulate. Folate, B12, B6, niacin, and riboflavin support nucleotide synthesis, methylation, NAD-dependent repair signaling, and redox balance.

Magnesium and zinc are key mineral cofactors for DNA replication and repair proteins. Keeping these nutrients adequate supports repair capacity after oxidative damage or copying errors, but it does not guarantee that every lesion is fixed.
Antioxidants, fat-soluble vitamins, and trace minerals here defend cells against oxidative damage, quiet chronic inflammation, and keep arteries and bones working correctly as you age.

Low selenium or copper over time can weaken thyroid conversion, immunity, and connective tissue; low polyphenols mean missing the plant compounds tied to lower cardiovascular and dementia risk in population studies.
Choline, carnitine, and betaine feed gut bacteria that make TMAO—see the TMAO balance section for precursors vs protectors (fiber, D and B vitamins, olive oil).

Probiotics (live cultures in yogurt, kefir, kimchi, sauerkraut) are not counted here—CFU amounts vary too much per product to log reliably; focus on regular fermented foods instead of chasing numbers.
Refined carbs and added sugar drive repeated glucose spikes that, over years, promote insulin resistance, fatty liver, weight gain, and higher diabetes and heart disease risk.
Fiber-rich whole foods digest slower, feed beneficial gut bacteria, and blunt the post-meal insulin surge that leaves you hungry again within hours.
Watching added sugar and refined flour—not just total carbs—helps prevent the metabolic pattern behind many preventable midlife health problems.

Phosphorus is required for life (ATP, DNA, bone), but when blood phosphate runs high—especially from cola, processed meats, and phosphate additives—the body lowers active vitamin D and shifts calcium away from bone.

That high-phosphate imbalance can harden arteries (vascular calcification) while bones weaken—a double hit seen with heavy soda, fast food, and additive-heavy packaged foods even when calcium and vitamin D intake looks fine on paper.
Whole-food phosphorus from meat, dairy, and legumes is rarely the problem; watch absorbable phosphate from additives. Kidney disease, heavy cola, and processed-meat diets need the closest attention.

When blood phosphate stays high despite diet changes, clinicians sometimes prescribe mealtime calcium acetate or other binders—synthetic products because food cannot deliver binder-strength doses. That is a monitored treatment, not a substitute for cutting additive phosphate.

Homocysteine (Hcy) is an amino acid in your blood that helps create proteins. Vitamin B12, vitamin B6, and folate (B9) break it down to generate other chemicals your body needs. When those cofactors are low, homocysteine accumulates—and high levels damage the arterial lining, accelerate vascular aging, and raise risk for stroke, endothelial dysfunction, and cognitive decline.

Why methylation matters here: methylation is a foundational biochemical process where a small methyl group (H₃C) is added to DNA, proteins, or other molecules. It occurs billions of times per second and governs health in several key ways—gene expression (silencing or activating genes so a single DNA set can produce different cell types and suppress mutated or viral DNA), mental health and brain function (building serotonin, dopamine, and melatonin), detoxification (allowing the liver to neutralize environmental toxins, heavy metals, and excess hormones like estrogen), cellular energy and repair (managing DNA repair, cellular aging, and creatine production for muscle energy), and cardiovascular health (recycling homocysteine so it does not accumulate and damage blood vessels).

Folate and B12 drive the main methylation pathway that recycles homocysteine back to methionine; B6 routes excess homocysteine toward glutathione-related sulfur metabolism; riboflavin supports FAD-dependent MTHFR activity, especially when folate handling is genetically slower. To function properly, this methylation cycle relies on an ongoing supply of folate, B12, B6, and choline from your diet.

Choline and betaine can support an alternate methylation route, but they overlap with TMAO biology. Food-level intake is useful; high-dose betaine supplements for homocysteine should be weighed against TMAO risk.

Potassium can help offset some sodium effects because it is linked with lower blood pressure, partly by helping balance sodium and relax blood vessel tension. In theory, a good target is about twice as much potassium as sodium while keeping sodium under the daily limit. This assumes no kidney disease and no ACE inhibitors or other medications that retain potassium.

There is not a strict magic ratio conventionally because the main focus is reducing sodium—it is easy to overeat. Guidelines usually recommend staying under 2,300 mg sodium daily, or closer to 1,500 mg if you already have high blood pressure. Potassium is often targeted around 3,500–4,700 mg/day.
Magnesium and fiber are core DASH-pattern nutrients—magnesium supports modest BP lowering in meta-analyses; fiber supports weight, gut health, and potassium-rich plant eating.

Food's naturally occurring nitrates get converted into nitric oxide, which helps widen blood vessels, improve blood flow, and lower blood pressure—both systemically and in the brain. Beets are among the best natural dietary sources of inorganic nitrates.

Flavonoids, polyphenols, and monounsaturated fat (olive-oil pattern) support endothelial function and align with Mediterranean/DASH eating. EPA and DHA from fish support vascular anti-inflammatory tone—in the brain as well as systemically.
Watch cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat too—unfavorable lipid patterns feed the same wear-and-tear pathway in systemic and cerebral small vessels.
Good potassium foods include fruits, beans, potatoes, leafy greens, and yogurt.

For sodium reduction, look up salt-swap strategies and herbs or condiments that can replace salt. Example: the same volume of Tajín usually has less sodium than the same volume of table salt because it is a chili-lime seasoning blend, not 100% salt.

You can also consider potassium-enriched salt alternatives, which replace some sodium with potassium. Potassium salts like Nu-Salt or Morton Salt Substitute use potassium chloride, which is sodium-free and mimics salty flavor, though they can have a bitter aftertaste. Avoid these if you have kidney disease or take certain medications.

However, you may not want to fully replace salt if you are low on iodine and use iodized salt as an easy iodine source. In that case you can use iodized salt and then use the alternatives at other times.

Lower-priority rows (vitamin D, vitamin K/K2, CoQ10) have modest, mixed, or mostly supplemental evidence for BP itself—they are still worth tracking in context but should not distract from sodium, potassium, plants, and fish. Use the DASH diet rows in this section for calcium tracking.
DASH (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) is an eating pattern built around minerals, fiber, and moderate saturated fat—not a single supplement stack.

The plan targets about 1,250 mg of calcium daily from 2 to 3 servings of low-fat dairy, dark leafy greens, and calcium-fortified foods; about 4,700 mg potassium from fruit, beans, potatoes, and greens; about 500 mg magnesium from nuts, legumes, whole grains, and leafy vegetables; and ample fiber from the vegetables, fruits, and whole grains that make up most of the pattern.

Calcium helps regulate smooth-muscle contraction in blood vessel walls; potassium and magnesium relax vessels and balance sodium; fiber supports weight, gut health, and potassium-rich plant eating. Keeping saturated fat moderate—lean proteins and low-fat dairy instead of fatty meats and full-fat cheese—aligns with the original DASH trials.

Food sources beat megadose calcium pills for most people. Spread calcium through the day, pair with vitamin D, and use the Read more tip for serving examples. Avoid potassium salt substitutes if you have kidney disease or take potassium-retaining medications.
foodSources: Lean poultry & fish over fatty red meat (lower sat fat)

enough: Higher blood pressure and worse lipids over years can damage small vessels in the brain.

Microvascular changes on a CT head indicate damage to the brain’s smallest blood vessels, often referred to as microvascular ischemic disease or small vessel disease. These changes typically represent the long-term effects of aging, chronic high blood pressure, or diabetes, and appear on a scan as wear and tear in the brain's white matter. In older adults, that pattern is often discussed alongside slower processing, word-finding trouble, and memory lapses that can look like ordinary aging but may partly reflect small-vessel injury over decades—not something food tracking alone can diagnose.

Nutrition for cerebral small vessels follows the same food pattern as systemic blood pressure—sodium, potassium, lipids, plants, nitrates, and fish oil matter for both. Keeping systemic pressure in range helps keep elevated pressure from reaching the brain’s smallest vessels.
Use Vascular - Blood Pressure for nutrient tracking; this section explains why cerebral small-vessel health belongs in longevity, not a separate nutrient list.

tooLow: Very low nitrate intake usually means few leafy greens, beets, or celery—missing an easy whole-food path to nitric oxide support for both systemic and brain blood vessels.
Heavy reliance on peeled, boiled, or nitrate-poor vegetables reduces this pathway even when overall vegetable intake looks adequate.
enough: Food's naturally occurring nitrates get converted into nitric oxide, which helps widen blood vessels, improve blood flow, and lower blood pressure—systemically and in the brain's small vessels.

Oral bacteria reduce dietary nitrate (NO₃) to nitrite; your body then converts it to nitric oxide (NO), which relaxes vessel smooth muscle. That pathway supports systemic blood pressure and cerebral small vessels alike—see Vascular - Blood Pressure for tracking.

Beets are among the best natural dietary sources of inorganic nitrates—beet-juice trials often use ~400 mg nitrate and show acute BP lowering. Spinach, arugula, celery, lettuce, and bok choy are also rich (often 100–250+ mg per 100 g). Reference intake here uses ~400 mg/day.
Chewing (not sipping through a straw that bypasses the mouth) and avoiding antibacterial mouthwash right before nitrate-rich meals may help the oral conversion step.

tooHigh: Ratios well below 2:1 usually mean sodium is high relative to potassium—even if potassium intake looks acceptable on its own.
Western diets often run 1:1 or worse because processed and restaurant foods add sodium while fruit and vegetable potassium lags.
enough: A potassium : sodium ratio around 2:1 or higher is a useful food-level check when sodium is kept under guideline limits—about twice as much potassium as sodium by weight.
This ratio is secondary to cutting sodium itself, which is the main lever guidelines emphasize for blood pressure.

Raising potassium from whole foods (fruit, beans, greens, yogurt) while lowering added salt improves the ratio without relying on potassium salt substitutes—important if you have kidney disease or take potassium-retaining medications.
foodSources: Raise potassium: fruit, beans, potatoes, greens
Lower sodium: cook at home, read labels
Salt swaps: herbs, citrus, chili-lime blends
Limit: deli meat, fast food, salty snacks
Potassium salt substitutes only if medically safe
Keep iodized salt if you need iodine

enough: TMAO isn't in food—gut microbes make it from the three precursors you see (choline, carnitine, betaine), and high TMAO promotes atherosclerosis. Nutrients and compounds that counter it work in three ways:
1. Block the bacterial enzyme (TMA-lyase): DMB from extra-virgin olive oil, balsamic and red-wine vinegar, grapeseed oil; allicin from raw garlic. These act on the bug, not just the dose of meat.

2. Reshape the microbiome away from TMA producers: fiber and prebiotics, polyphenols, flavonoids, and resveratrol (grapes, berries, tea, cocoa, onions, olive oil) shift gut populations toward lower TMAO over weeks.

3. Help the body process and clear it: vitamin D, B6, B12, and folate (D plus B vitamins lowered TMAO more than D alone in one year-long trial, especially when correcting deficiency), plus EPA and DHA fish oil for the vascular side. None replace eating less carnitine-rich red meat.

There is no single "TMAO reversal pill." Mediterranean-style eating (low red meat, olive oil, vinegar, legumes, fish, lots of plants) reliably lowers TMAO in trials. Probiotics and fermented foods (yogurt, kefir, kimchi, sauerkraut) may help but are strain-specific and hard to count—eat them regularly rather than chasing numbers.

Histamine tolerance belongs in longevity because quality of life matters: headaches, flushing, congestion, hives, sleep disruption, GI symptoms, and exercise intolerance can all affect daily function.

Food histamine is hard to score reliably because storage time, ripeness, aging, fermentation, alcohol, and leftovers change the dose. This section therefore tracks support nutrients rather than pretending every food has a stable histamine number.

Vitamin C supports histamine breakdown and antioxidant defense; B6 and copper support diamine oxidase biology; methionine-rich protein, folate, B12, and riboflavin support SAMe-dependent HNMT methylation; zinc supports immune and gut barrier function; magnesium, EPA/DHA, polyphenols, and flavonoids support inflammatory tone and mast-cell steadiness. These do not diagnose histamine intolerance, but they highlight diet gaps that can worsen tolerance.

Glycemic index (GI) and glycemic load (GL) measure different things. GI rates how quickly a standard carb portion of a food raises blood sugar—it is a property of the food itself. GL applies that speed to the carbs you actually eat: same GI, bigger portion means higher GL. GI describes the kind of carb; GL describes the impact of your real meal.

Mathematically, GL scales GI by the grams of carbohydrate in your serving. Multiply GI by carb grams, divide by 100. Example: white rice with GI 73 and 45 g carbs per cup → GL = 73 × 45 ÷ 100 ≈ 33 (high for one serving). Watermelon is the opposite case—GI around 76, but a small wedge has only a few grams of carbs, so GL stays low despite the high GI.

Formula: GL = (GI × grams of carbs per serving) ÷ 100. The ÷ 100 is a scaling step, not a guess: GI is scored on a 0–100 scale where glucose = 100, so it works like a percentage. Multiplying that percentage by grams without dividing would inflate the result 100×; dividing converts the scale back and keeps GL in a readable range (roughly single digits to low tens per serving—low ≤10, moderate 11–19, high ≥20). Daily GL is the sum of GL from everything you logged that day; this app shows your weekly average.
Large portions of bread, rice, fries, and sweets stack GL even when individual foods look acceptable on GI alone—raising diabetes and cardiovascular risk over time.
A week dominated by high-GI staples means repeated insulin surges that promote belly fat, fatty liver, and the fatigue–craving cycle that makes healthy eating harder.
Shifting toward beans, berries, intact grains, and smaller starch portions is one of the most direct dietary ways to prevent type 2 diabetes and metabolic decline.

tooLow: Low magnesium, vitamin B6, or calcium intake is linked with worse premenstrual mood swings, cramping, breast tenderness, and sleep disruption in several trials—not universal, but common enough to track.
Very low omega-3 (EPA/DHA) may add inflammatory tone that worsens luteal-phase symptoms when fish intake is rare.
enough: PMS & cycle balance belongs in longevity because decades of monthly symptom burden affects mood, sleep, work, and exercise consistency—not just a few bad days each month.

Magnesium supports muscle relaxation and nervous-system calm; vitamin B6 helps synthesize serotonin and GABA-related chemistry; calcium may blunt luteal-phase mood shifts; vitamin E supports breast tenderness in some studies; zinc and tryptophan feed hormone and neurotransmitter pathways when intake is low.

EPA and DHA from fatty fish add anti-inflammatory support that complements micronutrients. These repeat values from your micro and longevity entries so you can spot diet gaps—not a substitute for medical evaluation of endometriosis, PCOS, or severe PMDD.
foodSources: Magnesium — leafy greens, nuts, seeds, legumes, dark chocolate
Vitamin B6 — chickpeas, salmon, chicken, potatoes, bananas
Calcium — yogurt, cheese, fortified plant milk, leafy greens
Vitamin E — almonds, sunflower seeds, avocado, olive oil
Zinc — oysters, pumpkin seeds, beef, legumes
Tryptophan — turkey, eggs, soy, dairy, oats
EPA & DHA — salmon, sardines, mackerel, herring

tooLow: Premenopausal women lose iron every menstrual cycle—your dashboard uses 18 mg/day iron DV (vs 8 mg for men). Chronic low intake causes fatigue, cold intolerance, hair thinning, and can blunt thyroid and hormone balance.
Plant iron without vitamin C at the same meal absorbs poorly; tea and coffee at meals further block absorption.
enough: Iron & menstruation belongs in longevity because low iron is one of the most common correctable nutrient gaps in premenopausal women—and it affects energy, cognition, exercise tolerance, and thyroid cofactor status for decades.

Pair non-heme iron (beans, lentils, spinach, fortified grains) with vitamin C (citrus, peppers, berries) at the same meal. Folate, B12, riboflavin, and copper support red blood cell production alongside iron itself.
Heavy periods, athletes, and vegetarians often need deliberate food planning before supplements. Heme iron from lean meat and shellfish absorbs more easily but still benefits from a balanced plate.
foodSources: Heme iron — lean beef, lamb, shellfish, sardines
Non-heme iron — lentils, beans, spinach, tofu, fortified cereal
Vitamin C — citrus, bell peppers, strawberries, broccoli
Folate — leafy greens, legumes, asparagus, fortified grains
Vitamin B12 — fish, eggs, dairy, fortified nutritional yeast
Copper — nuts, seeds, shellfish, dark chocolate

tooLow: Low fiber and infrequent cruciferous vegetables mean fewer tools for clearing estrogen metabolites through the gut and liver pathways.
Low sulforaphane usually means broccoli, kale, and Brussels sprouts are rare—missing one of the best-studied food compounds for healthy estrogen metabolism.
enough: Estrogen metabolism belongs in longevity because how you break down and clear estrogen metabolites affects breast tissue, cycle symptoms, and long-term hormone balance—not just reproductive years.
Fiber binds excess estrogen in the gut so it is excreted rather than reabsorbed. Folate and vitamin B6 support methylation steps in liver estrogen clearance.

Sulforaphane from broccoli, Brussels sprouts, kale, and cabbage activates Nrf2 antioxidant defenses and supports healthier estrogen metabolite ratios in research—not by blocking all estrogen, but by helping process it. Chop crucifers and let them sit 5–10 minutes before cooking to maximize sulforaphane; broccoli sprouts are especially rich.
foodSources: Vitamin B6 — chickpeas, salmon, potatoes, bananas

tooLow: After menopause, ovarian estrogen falls sharply—bone loss accelerates, hot flashes and sleep disruption may persist, and cardiovascular risk shifts toward patterns more common in men.
Low calcium, vitamin D, magnesium, or vitamin K speeds bone loss when estrogen no longer protects trabecular bone.
enough: Post-menopause belongs in longevity because the hormone transition changes bone, heart, brain, and body-composition trajectories for decades—not a brief adjustment period.
Calcium, vitamin D, magnesium, and vitamin K work together for bone mineralization and muscle function; see Bone density for the same tracked values in another context.
Fiber and polyphenols support cardiometabolic health when estrogen's vascular protection fades. EPA and DHA from fish support heart and brain aging.
Sulforaphane from crucifers still supports estrogen metabolite balance when ovarian production is low—several weekly servings of broccoli, kale, or cabbage remain practical targets.
foodSources: Calcium — yogurt, cheese, fortified plant milk, leafy greens
Vitamin D — fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified dairy
Magnesium — nuts, seeds, legumes, leafy greens
Vitamin K — kale, spinach, broccoli, natto

tooLow: Low zinc, magnesium, or vitamin D intake is linked with lower testosterone production and poorer free-testosterone balance in observational and intervention studies—especially when combined with very low fat intake or excess body fat.
Chronic calorie deficit and ultra-low-fat diets can suppress androgen signaling even when micronutrients look adequate on paper.
enough: Testosterone support belongs in longevity because androgen tone affects muscle mass, bone density, mood, metabolic rate, and body composition across decades—not just libido.

Zinc is concentrated in gonadal tissue and supports testosterone synthesis; magnesium supports free testosterone by reducing sex-hormone-binding globulin in some trials; vitamin D supports androgen receptor signaling; selenium provides antioxidant defense; vitamin B6 supports steroid hormone metabolism.

Monounsaturated fats from olive oil and avocado support a healthier fat pattern than excess saturated fat. EPA and DHA modulate inflammation that can interfere with Leydig cell function. These track diet gaps—not a substitute for medical evaluation of hypogonadism.
foodSources: Zinc — oysters, beef, pumpkin seeds, legumes
Magnesium — nuts, seeds, leafy greens, legumes
Vitamin D — fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified dairy
Selenium — Brazil nuts, fish, eggs, sunflower seeds
Vitamin B6 — chicken, salmon, chickpeas, potatoes
Monounsaturated fat — olive oil, avocado, almonds

tooLow: Infrequent cruciferous vegetables means low sulforaphane—one of the best-studied food compounds for prostate cell defense in human and cell research.
Low selenium, vitamin D, or zinc may reduce antioxidant and regulatory support in prostate tissue when intake is chronically low.
enough: Prostate health belongs in longevity because benign enlargement and cancer risk rise with age—nutrition cannot replace screening, but diet patterns matter over decades.

Sulforaphane from broccoli, Brussels sprouts, and kale activates Nrf2 pathways linked to prostate cell defense in research. Chop broccoli and let it sit before cooking; broccoli sprouts are especially concentrated.

Selenium, vitamin D, and zinc concentrate in prostate tissue and support antioxidant and cell-regulation pathways. Vitamin E contributes oxidative defense. EPA and DHA add anti-inflammatory support. Track food gaps here alongside regular medical care.

tooLow: Low fiber and infrequent crucifers reduce tools for clearing estrogen metabolites—relevant for men because aromatase converts testosterone to estrogen, especially with belly fat and aging.
Low zinc may worsen aromatase balance when intake is chronically low.
enough: Estrogen balance belongs in longevity for men because relative estrogen excess—not just low testosterone—drives gynecomastia, belly fat, and lower androgen tone as aromatase activity rises with age and adiposity.
Sulforaphane from broccoli and crucifers supports healthier estrogen metabolite ratios via Nrf2 and liver detox pathways. Fiber binds metabolites in the gut; zinc supports aromatase balance.

Body composition still dominates—nutrition supports the pathways, but excess visceral fat remains the main driver of aromatization. These repeat tracked values so you can spot diet gaps alongside strength training and weight management.
